---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 9: Simultaneous equations models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 9](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-9-simultaneous-equations-models/).

Source text: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*, 2nd ed., MIT Press.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

```{julia}
mroz = dataset("wooldridge/mroz")     # 753 x 22
size(mroz)
```

The chapter estimates a labor-supply / wage-offer system on the working
subsample (`lwage` observed), i.e. 428 of the 753 women.

```{julia}
mz = DataFrames.dropmissing(mroz,
        [:lwage, :hours, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6,
         :nwifeinc, :exper, :expersq])
DataFrames.nrow(mz)
```

### A note on `ivreg2` standard errors

The UCLA page uses the user-written `ivreg2`. With no `small` option `ivreg2`
reports **large-sample** (divisor `N`) standard errors and `z` statistics,
whereas StatEcon's `stata_ivregress_2sls` (built on `FixedEffectModels`) uses
the small-sample divisor `N − K`. Coefficients are unaffected; standard errors
differ by exactly `sqrt((N-K)/N)`. The helper below rescales a fitted 2SLS
model to the `ivreg2` convention so the published numbers can be checked
digit-for-digit.

```{julia}
function ivreg2_table(m; dep::AbstractString = "y")
    β  = StatsBase.coef(m)
    n  = Int(StatsBase.nobs(m))
    k  = length(β)
    se = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(StatsBase.vcov(m))) .* sqrt((n - k) / n)
    z  = β ./ se
    p  = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Normal(), abs.(z)))
    nm = replace.(string.(StatsBase.coefnames(m)), "(Intercept)" => "_cons")
    Printf.@printf("%-10s %13s %12s %8s %8s\n", dep, "Coef.", "Std. Err.", "z", "P>|z|")
    println(repeat("-", 55))
    for i in eachindex(β)
        Printf.@printf("%-10s %13.6g %12.6g %8.2f %8.3f\n", nm[i], β[i], se[i], z[i], p[i])
    end
    return (; β, se, z, p, names = nm)
end
```

`ivreg2` also prints a **Sargan** overidentification statistic. StatEcon ships
`stata_estat_overid_2sls` for the single-endogenous-regressor case; Example 9.6
has two endogenous regressors, so the small helper below computes the same
`n·R²` statistic for the general case.

```{julia}
function sargan(df, y, endog, exog, ivs)
    n  = DataFrames.nrow(df)
    Z  = hcat(ones(n), [Float64.(df[!, v]) for v in vcat(exog, ivs)]...)
    Xe = hcat([Float64.(df[!, v]) for v in endog]...)
    X  = hcat(ones(n), Z * (Z \ Xe), [Float64.(df[!, v]) for v in exog]...)
    Xr = hcat(ones(n), [Float64.(df[!, v]) for v in vcat(endog, exog)]...)
    yv = Float64.(df[!, y])
    u  = yv .- Xr * (X \ yv)
    r  = u .- Z * (Z \ u)
    R2 = 1 - sum(abs2, r) / sum(abs2, u)
    chi2 = n * R2
    dfree = length(ivs) - length(endog)
    pv = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Chisq(dfree), chi2)
    Printf.@printf("Sargan statistic (overidentification test of all instruments): %8.3f\n", chi2)
    Printf.@printf("                                        Chi-sq(%d) P-val = %9.5f\n", dfree, pv)
    return (; chi2, df = dfree, p = pv)
end
```

## Example 9.5 (mroz.dta) — labor supply and the wage offer

Original Stata code:

```{julia}
#=
use mroz, clear
reg hours lwage educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc
ivreg2 hours educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc (lwage= exper expersq)
reg hours educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc exper expersq if lwage~=.
test age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc
ivreg2 lwage educ exper expersq (hours=educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc)
reg lwage educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc exper expersq
test age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc
=#
```

### (1) OLS hours equation

```{julia}
ols_hours = stata_regress(mz; y = :hours,
                          x = [:lwage, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match with Stata: N = 428, F(6, 421) = 5.04,
R² = 0.0670, Root MSE = 755.16; `lwage` −17.40781 (54.21544),
`kidslt6` −342.5048 (100.0059), `kidsge6` −115.0205 (30.82925),
`_cons` 2114.697 (340.1307).

### (2) 2SLS hours equation, `lwage` instrumented by `exper`, `expersq`

```{julia}
iv_hours = stata_ivregress_2sls(mz, :hours,
              [:lwage] => [:exper, :expersq],
              [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc];
              robust = false, first = false)
```

Rescaled to the `ivreg2` (large-sample) convention:

```{julia}
ivreg2_table(iv_hours.iv; dep = "hours")
sargan(mz, :hours, [:lwage], [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc],
       [:exper, :expersq])
```

**Comparison.** Coefficients match Stata exactly: `lwage` 1544.819,
`educ` −177.449, `age` −10.78409, `kidslt6` −210.8339, `kidsge6` −47.55708,
`nwifeinc` −9.249121, `_cons` 2432.198. The rescaled standard errors reproduce
`ivreg2`'s 476.7913, 57.66517, 9.498705, 175.4811, 56.45049, 6.427897,
589.2929. The Sargan statistic 0.858 with Chi-sq(1) p = 0.35425 matches the
published value exactly. (The unscaled `stata_ivregress_2sls` table prints
480.74, 58.14, … — the same numbers divided by `sqrt(421/428)`.)

### (3) Reduced form for `hours`, and the test of the excluded instruments

```{julia}
rf_hours = stata_regress(mz; y = :hours,
             x = [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc, :exper, :expersq])
```

```{julia}
stata_test(rf_hours, ["age", "kidslt6", "kidsge6", "nwifeinc"])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match: N = 428, F(7, 420) = 9.79, R² = 0.1403,
Root MSE = 725.73, `exper` 47.00509 (14.55649), `expersq` −0.5136442
(0.4373576). The joint test gives F(4, 420) = 4.80, Prob > F = 0.0009 —
identical to Stata.

### (4) 2SLS wage equation, `hours` instrumented

`ivreg2 lwage educ exper expersq (hours=educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc)`
lists `educ` both as an included exogenous regressor and as an instrument;
Stata reports *"Collinearities detected among instruments: 1 instrument(s)
dropped"*. The effective excluded instruments are `age kidslt6 kidsge6
nwifeinc`, which is what the Julia call specifies.

```{julia}
iv_wage = stata_ivregress_2sls(mz, :lwage,
             [:hours] => [:age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc],
             [:educ, :exper, :expersq];
             robust = false, first = false)
```

```{julia}
ivreg2_table(iv_wage.iv; dep = "lwage")
sargan(mz, :lwage, [:hours], [:educ, :exper, :expersq],
       [:age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc])
```

**Comparison.** Coefficients match exactly: `hours` 0.0001608, `educ` 0.1111175,
`exper` 0.032646, `expersq` −0.0006765, `_cons` −0.69279. The rescaled standard
errors reproduce `ivreg2`'s 0.0002141, 0.0152421, 0.0179552, 0.00044, 0.3048041.
Sargan 2.941, Chi-sq(3) p = 0.40084 — exactly the published values.

### (5) OLS wage equation and the second exclusion test

```{julia}
ols_wage = stata_regress(mz; y = :lwage,
             x = [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc, :exper, :expersq])
```

```{julia}
stata_test(ols_wage, ["age", "kidslt6", "kidsge6", "nwifeinc"])
```

**Comparison.** Exact match: N = 428, F(7, 420) = 11.78, R² = 0.1641,
Root MSE = 0.66669; `educ` 0.0998844 (0.0150975), `exper` 0.0407097
(0.0133723), `_cons` −0.3579972 (0.3182963). Joint test F(4, 420) = 0.91,
Prob > F = 0.4555 — identical to Stata.

### Extra IV diagnostics (not on the UCLA page)

```{julia}
estat_firststage(mz, :hours, [:lwage], [:exper, :expersq],
                 [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc])
```

```{julia}
estat_endogenous(mz, :hours, [:lwage], [:exper, :expersq],
                 [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc]; robust = false)
```

## Example 9.6 (mroz.dta) — a nonlinear-in-endogenous-variable hours equation

Original Stata code:

```{julia}
#=
gen lwagesq = lwage^2
gen agesq=age^2
gen educsq = educ^2
gen nwifeincsq = nwifeinc^2
ivreg2 hours educ age kidslt6 kidsge6 nwifeinc (lwage lwagesq = exper expersq agesq educsq nwifeincsq)
=#
```

```{julia}
mz.lwagesq    = mz.lwage .^ 2
mz.agesq      = Float64.(mz.age) .^ 2
mz.educsq     = Float64.(mz.educ) .^ 2
mz.nwifeincsq = Float64.(mz.nwifeinc) .^ 2

iv96 = stata_ivregress_2sls(mz, :hours,
          [:lwage, :lwagesq] => [:exper, :expersq, :agesq, :educsq, :nwifeincsq],
          [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc];
          robust = false, first = false)
```

```{julia}
ivreg2_table(iv96.iv; dep = "hours")
sargan(mz, :hours, [:lwage, :lwagesq],
       [:educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc],
       [:exper, :expersq, :agesq, :educsq, :nwifeincsq])
```

**Comparison.** All eight coefficients match Stata to every printed digit:
`lwage` 1873.62, `lwagesq` −437.2911, `educ` −87.8511, `age` −9.142303,
`kidslt6` −185.0554, `kidsge6` −58.18949, `nwifeinc` −7.233422,
`_cons` 1657.926. The rescaled standard errors reproduce `ivreg2`'s 630.0193,
346.7893, 65.76994, 8.492916, 160.757, 49.63412, 5.751229, 769.9772. Sargan
2.612 with Chi-sq(3) p = 0.45537 — exactly as published.

## Systems view: `reg3` (3SLS)

The UCLA page estimates the two structural equations one at a time. StatEcon's
`stata_reg3` fits them jointly by three-stage least squares; the results below
are *not* on the UCLA page and are included only to show the system command.

```{julia}
reg3_sys = stata_reg3(mz,
             :hours => [:lwage, :educ, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6, :nwifeinc],
             :lwage => [:hours, :educ, :exper, :expersq])
nothing
```
